Intro1: Metacognition is about being self-aware of your own thinking. It includes noticing the assumptions you are making or understanding how you reached a particular conclusion. It also involves recognising instances when you might slip up and making appropriate adjustments. This can happen when you rush, rely on vivid examples, or accept a tidy story too quickly (Flavell, 1979; Dunlosky & Metcalfe, 2009). All these instances are potentially exacerbated by AI use.
Intro2: Metacognitive skills can be supported by habits that make thinking more visible. For example, you can check what evidence a conclusion depends on, or be careful to separate observation from interpretation. You can also keep track of what can and cannot be inferred from limited information (Nelson & Narens, 1990; Kuhn, 1999). Exercises that prompt this kind of reflection can encourage a more deliberate approach to evaluating arguments and explanations. This is especially useful when encountering rapidly produced AI materials that look persuasive but may have serious conceptual or logical errors.
Ref_Flavell: Flavell, J. H. (1979). Metacognition and cognitive monitoring: A new area of cognitive–developmental inquiry. American Psychologist, 34(10), 906–911.
Ref_Nelson: Nelson, T. O., & Narens, L. (1990). Metamemory: A theoretical framework and new findings. The Psychology of Learning and Motivation, 26, 125–173.
Ref_Kuhn: Kuhn, D. (1999). A developmental model of critical thinking. Educational Researcher, 28(2), 16–25.
